content.claim: School closures in Sweden (2013–2020) demonstrated measurable negative political effects: closures decreased trust in municipal politicians and reduced electoral support for incumbents, with effects most pronounced for left-leaning parties, suggesting political costs may drive local resistance to closures independent of educational rationale
content.context: Fresh live research through the International comparison lens
limitations: Study measures electoral/trust effects rather than mechanisms for preventing closures or retaining schools
Swedish context (locally elected school boards with taxing power) differs structurally from centralized systems like post-2013 Hungary
Finding does not address whether political cost translates into sustainable school retention policies or only temporary delays
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
